title: Correctness, Performance, Visibility. Pick Three.
summary: How a Rust port became a deeper exercise in designing the boundaries between a matching engine, an exchange, and a real-time UI.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Back in 2022, I got my first exposure to professional software engineering through finance, and it completely changed how I viewed the industry.

At [BAM](https://www.bamfunds.com/), I saw analysts, developers, and engineers all using code to solve real problems. That challenged the picture I had in my head of finance as mostly spreadsheets and reports. Instead, I found an environment where software sat close to research, trading, risk, and decision-making.

What stayed with me most was learning about quantitative developers: engineers building systems that operate close to the execution of trades. I was drawn to the idea that markets are not just abstract financial systems, but real technical systems made of orderbooks, matching engines, protocols, latency constraints, and infrastructure decisions. That curiosity is what eventually led me to build my own matching engine in Rust.

sections.heading: Why Rust?
sections.body:
It is hard to pin the choice down to one reason, but the main motivation was that I wanted to become a more systems-minded developer. C++ still dominates many low-latency trading systems, and that is not changing anytime soon, but Rust gave me a way to explore similar problems while being forced to think carefully about ownership, mutation, and memory layout.

I did not build this project because Rust magically makes matching engines easy. In some places, it made the implementation more awkward. But that awkwardness was useful. The borrow checker forced me to be explicit about when I was reading from the book, when I was mutating it, and which part of the system actually owned each piece of state.

sections.heading: What's an Orderbook
sections.body: Here's the crux of the write up. At a high level, an orderbook is a collection of all buy and sell orders for some security, sorted by price.

Bids represent people willing to buy, Asks represent people willing to sell. Sorting by price is relevant because bids and asks get matched when the best (highest) bid is >= the best (lowest) ask.

It's also important to note that most orderbooks operate under the idea of price-time priority. Matches occur first at the best prices, then at the earliest arrivals.

sections.heading: Core Data Structures
sections.body:
Bids and asks are represented using `BTreeMap<Price, Vec<OrderId>>` where each price level maps to a queue of order IDs resting at that price.

This choice fits the shape of an orderbook. Prices are not just values to look up; they form a sorted ladder. The engine constantly needs to ask questions like: what is the highest bid, what is the lowest ask, and which price levels are executable for this incoming order?

A regular `HashMap` would make exact price lookups easy, but it would not preserve order. To find the best bid or ask, the engine would have to scan through all price levels. A `BTreeMap` keeps prices sorted, so the book can efficiently access the lowest ask and highest bid without searching through every level.

There is also an interesting memory-layout reason this structure is appealing. A traditional binary search tree stores one key per node, which can lead to lots of heap allocations and pointer chasing. Rust’s BTreeMap stores multiple keys per node, which better matches how modern CPUs like to read memory: in contiguous chunks. In practice, that means a B-tree can trade a few extra comparisons inside a node for fewer cache misses and better locality.

sections.caption: rough idea, more nuance in rust docs

sections.body:
Orders themselves are stored separately in a `HashMap<OrderId, Order>`, while an additional index, `HashMap<OrderId, OrderEntry>`, provides constant-time access to an order's position within the book for modification and cancellation. Rather than relying on a single data structure, the engine maintains multiple views of the same state, each optimized for a different access pattern.

Within each price level, orders are stored in a dynamic array rather than a doubly linked list. This sacrifices worst-case `O(n)` deletions in favor of a simpler implementation and the cache locality benefits discussed earlier.

Finally, `HashMap<Price, LevelData>` stores aggregate information such as the total quantity and order count at each price level. This avoids repeatedly scanning an entire queue and becomes useful for certain order types discussed later.

sections.heading: The Matching Loop
sections.body:
The matching engine is less mysterious than it sounds. At its core, it repeatedly compares the best bid and best ask. If the highest buyer is willing to pay at least as much as the lowest seller is asking, a trade can happen.

Each iteration does a small number of things:
We'll take a look at the code as well because the borrow checker informs the order that operations need to happen in the matching loop.

Here's we initalize resulting `trades` and `filled_orders`, which we'll aggregate later to created our `MatchResult`. We start a loop, and we do our common sense checks and get our necessary info.

A big part of Rust's ownership model is it's rules around mutable and immutable references. At any given time you can have any number of immutable references or one mutable reference but not both. Take a look at the following:

Reads and writes to the orders map happen seperately within thier own scope to ensure both types of references to the don't exist at the same time.

After this, we check to see if either order was filled, remove them from the book if they are, and return the results.

sections.heading: Flavors of Limit Order
sections.body:
While on the topic of matching and orders heres some common order types I decided to cover.

`GoodTilCancel`: Fill what you can, rest the remainder

`FillAndKill`: Fill what you can, cancel the remainder

`FillOrKill`:  Only execute if the full quantity is avaliable

`Market`: Execute immediately against avaliable liquidity

`FillOrKill` exposed a useful optimization boundary. To decide whether an order can fully execute, the engine does not need to inspect every individual resting order. It only needs to know whether there is enough aggregate liquidity across executable price levels.

Imagine 1,000 orders sitting at the same price level. A naive `can_fully_fill` implementation could walk every order at that level and sum the remaining quantity one by one. But if the book already maintains aggregate level data, the engine can treat that entire level as one number.

That made per-level data more than a UI convenience. It became part of the matching engine’s validation path.

sections.heading: A Body For a Brain
sections.body: The orderbook is the brain of the system: it knows how to store orders, match them, and preserve price-time priority. But an exchange needs more than a brain. It needs a body around it: something that receives commands, assigns order IDs, validates requests, emits events, and exposes the book to the outside world.

In this project, the `Exchange` owns the `Orderbook` and serves as the public interface into it. Instead of having external code reach directly into the book and mutate bids, asks, or order queues, the exchange exposes a smaller command surface:

That boundary matters because clients should send intent, not implementation details. A UI, simulation, or server task should not need to know how the orderbook stores price levels or where an order lives in a queue. It should be able to say, “submit this order” or “cancel this order,” and let the exchange translate that into the correct book operation.

The exchange also owns information that does not belong in the orderbook itself. For example, the book does not need to know how order IDs are assigned. It only needs a valid order ID once an order reaches it. The exchange can handle that identity layer, call into the book, and then turn the result into events like `OrderAccepted`, `TradeExecuted`, `OrderRemoved`, or `Snapshot`.

That made the exchange layer more than a thin wrapper. It became the boundary between internal state and external behavior.

sections.heading: When the Wrong Layer Owns Time
sections.body:
One problem that made this boundary clearer was order pruning.

My first approach tried to make pruning a [responsibility of the orderbook itself](https://github.com/Scriptize/Orderbook/commit/7e372e3f9f19a451047ff62f0eb1f161241743c4). Since `GoodForDay` orders eventually need to expire, I started thinking of the book as something that should manage its own background cleanup. That pushed me toward synchronization primitives around the book and even around individual orders. At one point, the design involved an [inner/outer orderbook locking pattern](https://github.com/Scriptize/Orderbook/commit/22f6cc4085b967d6ab14fbe114de624726085d3c) using `Arc<Mutex<...>>`

sections.caption: this is all so silly in retrospect 😹

sections.body:
That made cross-thread access possible, but it pushed too much complexity into the orderbook. Simple operations now had to think about locking, shared ownership, and thread coordination. The matching logic became harder to reason about, not because matching changed, but because the book was being asked to manage a scheduling problem.

The cleaner realization was that pruning is not an orderbook concern. The orderbook should know how to remove an order correctly, but it should not own a background thread or decide when time-based cleanup runs. Expiration is exchange/server policy; removal is orderbook mechanics.

sections.caption: now watch as your GFDs disappear!

sections.body:
A better design is for a server-level task to track time, send expired-order requests through the exchange, and let the orderbook perform removals through its normal command path. Here's [the commit](https://github.com/Scriptize/Orderbook/commit/ff3c07e9f2f42202b8c8c05cce268945786bd262) that does that, almost a year after [pruning was introduced!](https://github.com/Scriptize/Orderbook/commit/aa4efbf185fe477b1f2616139cd45f8d37be7ece)

This avoided pushing `Arc<Mutex<...>>` through the entire book and kept the matching engine simple: given a valid operation, mutate state and return the result. The lesson was that synchronization can solve access, but it can also reveal a boundary problem. In this case, the orderbook did not need more locks. It needed to own less.

sections.heading: Matching Engine to Market Simulator
sections.body: Once the exchange could emit events and snapshots, the project stopped being just a matching engine and started becoming a simulator. The server, UI, and actors each sit outside the orderbook, but they all depend on the same boundary: commands go in, events and snapshots come out.

The server is one of the shortest pieces of the project because its role is narrow. The orderbook handles matching, the exchange handles lifecycle and events, and the server handles networking. It listens for TCP connections, upgrades them to WebSockets, waits for clients to subscribe, and streams exchange output to anyone watching.

Tokio’s `broadcast` channel fits this nicely. Each exchange event can be sent once and received by multiple subscribers, which means the server does not need to manually track every connected client. The interesting part is what happens when a client falls behind. Since the channel is bounded, Tokio can report `RecvError::Lagged` when old messages are dropped before a receiver sees them.

sections.caption: had to slow down message sending a bit

sections.body: In this project, that becomes a useful recovery point. If a client misses events, the server can send a fresh snapshot instead of expecting the UI to reconstruct the book from an incomplete event stream. Events explain what happened, but snapshots restore what is true now.

sections.caption: ui with some metrics and actor placed orders

sections.body:
The final piece is actors. Actors are simulated participants that observe the book (via snapshots) and produce commands. A market maker can place bids and asks around the mid-price, noise traders can submit random orders, and takers can cross the spread with market orders. Each actor follows the same rule as an external client: it sends intent through the exchange instead of mutating the orderbook directly.

The actor model is still something I'm actively working on. I imagine a more mature version where you can configure the number of actors as well as their type and then observe what the book looks like under those conditions.

sections.heading: Takeaways and Final Thoughts
sections.body:
The biggest lesson from this project was not just how to build an orderbook. It was how much cleaner a system becomes when each layer owns one concern and exposes a narrow boundary to the rest of the world.

The orderbook should not care about WebSockets, actors, dashboards, or simulation settings. It should accept commands, update state, and produce results. The exchange can handle lifecycle and events. The server can handle networking. The UI can observe snapshots. Once those boundaries were clearer, correctness, performance, and visibility stopped feeling like competing goals. They became separate things I could reason about without dragging the entire project into every change.

There is also a Rust-shaped lesson here. The borrow checker forces you to ask who owns data, who can mutate it, and where access is allowed. This project made me ask the same questions at the design level: which layer owns matching, which layer owns simulation, which layer owns broadcasting, and which layers are only allowed to observe? A lot of the mistakes I made came from letting those answers blur.

I still want to keep pushing this further, especially around configurable simulations and benchmarking. I’m curious how the book behaves as I change the core data structures and measure the tradeoffs directly. But the main thing I’m taking with me is the intuition that good boundaries are not just organization. They are what let a system grow without every new feature turning into a rewrite.
